edges.description: Merlin loss releases downstream Hippo/YAP/TAZ-TEAD proliferative programs.
NF2-null schwannoma growth is linked to pro-angiogenic VEGF biology.
NF2-related schwannoma biology creates a rationale for mTORC1-directed therapy.
Tumor growth on the eighth cranial nerve disrupts auditory and vestibular function.
Cochlear nerve involvement produces hearing impairment.
Eighth-nerve tumor effects can produce persistent tinnitus.
Vestibular nerve involvement produces vertigo or disequilibrium.

edges.source: Everolimus for Progressive NF2-Related Vestibular Schwannoma

nodes.description: Bevacizumab is an off-label anti-VEGF pharmacotherapy option for selected NF2-related vestibular schwannomas, with reported tumor-volume and hearing benefits in observational literature but frequent adverse events.

evidence.reference_title: Bevacizumab for Vestibular Schwannomas in Neurofibromatosis Type 2: A Systematic Review of Tumor Control and Hearing Preservation.
evidence.snippet: Bevacizumab showed a partial tumor volume reduction (≥20%) in 40% of cases and disease stabilization in 50%, while 10% experienced tumor progression.

evidence.reference_title: Bevacizumab Treatment for Patients with NF2-Related Schwannomatosis: A Single Center Experience.
evidence.snippet: Following treatment, 40% of the patients experienced hearing improvement, 53%, stable hearing, and 7%, hearing loss.

nodes.description: Everolimus has been evaluated as mTORC1-directed pharmacotherapy for progressive NF2-related vestibular schwannoma; evidence supports possible growth-rate reduction but not objective radiographic response in the small phase II cohort.
nodes.id: Everolimus for Progressive NF2-Related Vestibular Schwannoma

evidence.reference_title: Imaging as an early biomarker to predict sensitivity to everolimus for progressive NF2-related vestibular schwannoma.
evidence.snippet: After 52 weeks of treatment, the median annual VS growth rate decreased from 77.2% at baseline to 29.4%.

nodes.description: Microsurgery offers tumor removal and local control but must be balanced against risks to hearing and adjacent cranial nerves.

evidence.reference_title: Long-Term Hearing Outcome For Vestibular Schwannomas After Microsurgery And Radiotherapy: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis.
evidence.snippet: Microsurgery demonstrated a higher prevalence of maintaining long-term serviceable hearing, with a pooled estimate of 74.5% (95% CI: 63.5%-84.1%).

nodes.description: Loss or dysfunction of NF2/merlin is the central upstream molecular event in NF2-related vestibular schwannoma and a recurrent driver in schwannoma biology. Merlin normally coordinates contact, proliferation, and motility signals; loss of this restraint promotes tumorigenesis.

evidence.reference_title: NF2-Related Schwannomatosis (NF2): Molecular Insights and Therapeutic Avenues.
evidence.snippet: Merlin, a tumor suppressor, integrates multiple signaling pathways that regulate cell contact, proliferation, and motility, thereby influencing tumor growth.

evidence.reference_title: NF2-Related Schwannomatosis (NF2): Molecular Insights and Therapeutic Avenues.
evidence.snippet: The loss of Merlin disrupts these pathways, leading to tumorigenesis.

meta.association: Causal tumor suppressor gene in NF2-related disease and a key schwannoma driver.

evidence.reference_title: NF2-Related Schwannomatosis (NF2): Molecular Insights and Therapeutic Avenues.
evidence.snippet: This review consolidates the current knowledge on NF2 syndrome, emphasizing the molecular pathology associated with the mutations in the gene of the same name, the NF2 gene, and the subsequent dysfunction of its product, the Merlin protein.

nodes.description: Human primary schwannoma cells were used to test YAP/TAZ-driven TEAD activity in NF2-null schwannoma growth.

evidence.reference_title: Inhibition of YAP/TAZ-driven TEAD activity prevents growth of NF2-null schwannoma and meningioma.
evidence.snippet: driven by the TAZ protein in human and mouse NF2-null schwannoma cells

nodes.description: Proton beam therapy has high reported tumor-control rates but does not clearly improve hearing or facial-nerve preservation compared with many modern stereotactic radiosurgery series.

evidence.reference_title: Proton beam radiation therapy for vestibular schwannomas-tumor control and hearing preservation rates: a systematic review and meta-analysis.
evidence.snippet: Proton beam therapy for VSs achieves high tumor control rates, as high as 95.4%.

nodes.description: Hearing impairment is one of the most common and functionally important manifestations of acoustic neuroma and may reflect tumor effects on the cochlear nerve or treatment effects.

evidence.reference_title: Long-Term Hearing Outcome For Vestibular Schwannomas After Microsurgery And Radiotherapy: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis.
evidence.snippet: Hearing loss is a common symptom associated with vestibular schwannoma (VS), either because of the tumor's effects on the cochlear nerve or due to active treatments such as surgery or stereotactic radiosurgery (SRS).

nodes.description: Stereotactic radiosurgery is an active tumor-control strategy for selected acoustic neuromas and may preserve nerve function better than microsurgery in some treatment comparisons.

evidence.reference_title: Treatment options for unilateral vestibular schwannoma: a network meta-analysis.
evidence.snippet: In patients with VS, MS and radiosurgery showed better local tumor control rates; however, compared with MS, different SRS all provided better protection of nerve function and improved the symptoms of vestibular function and tinnitus, among which the best was SRS.

nodes.description: Tinnitus can be persistent, distressing, and quality-of-life limiting in patients with vestibular schwannoma.

evidence.reference_title: Vestibular Schwannoma and Tinnitus: A Systematic Review of Microsurgery Compared to Gamma Knife Radiosurgery.
evidence.snippet: Although VS can cause a variety of symptoms, tinnitus is one of the most distressing symptoms for patients and can greatly impact quality of life.

nodes.description: VEGF-related angiogenic signaling contributes to NF2-related vestibular schwannoma treatment biology and provides the mechanistic rationale for bevacizumab.

evidence.reference_title: Bevacizumab for Vestibular Schwannomas in Neurofibromatosis Type 2: A Systematic Review of Tumor Control and Hearing Preservation.
evidence.snippet: Bevacizumab, a VEGF-targeting monoclonal antibody, has emerged as a less invasive treatment option, showing potential for tumor volume reduction and hearing preservation.

nodes.description: Disequilibrium and vertigo are common vestibular manifestations and are treatment-relevant outcomes in unilateral vestibular schwannoma.

evidence.reference_title: Treatment options for unilateral vestibular schwannoma: a network meta-analysis.
evidence.snippet: In terms of improving the rate of disequilibrium/vertigo, the order from the highest to the lowest was SRS, Observation, FSRT 3 fractions, FSRT 5 fractions, MS, and ConFSRT.

nodes.description: Tumor growth along cranial nerve VIII and in the internal auditory canal/cerebellopontine-angle region impairs auditory and vestibular signaling; larger tumors can affect adjacent cranial nerves and posterior fossa structures.

evidence.reference_title: Vestibular Schwannoma and Tinnitus: A Systematic Review of Microsurgery Compared to Gamma Knife Radiosurgery.
evidence.snippet: Although VS can cause a variety of symptoms, tinnitus is one of the most distressing symptoms for patients and can greatly impact quality of life.

nodes.description: NF2-null schwannoma cells depend on Hippo-pathway effector activity, including YAP/TAZ-driven TEAD transcriptional output, to sustain tumor-cell growth. This mechanism is experimentally targetable in human primary tumor cells and mouse models.

biological_processes: positive regulation of Schwann cell proliferation

evidence.reference_title: Inhibition of YAP/TAZ-driven TEAD activity prevents growth of NF2-null schwannoma and meningioma.
evidence.snippet: driven by the TAZ protein in human and mouse NF2-null schwannoma cells

evidence.reference_title: Inhibition of YAP/TAZ-driven TEAD activity prevents growth of NF2-null schwannoma and meningioma.
evidence.snippet: successful use of TEAD palmitoylation inhibitors in a preclinical mouse model of schwannoma points to their potential future clinical use.

nodes.description: mTORC1 signaling is implicated as a downstream actionable pathway in progressive NF2-related vestibular schwannoma, motivating everolimus therapy.

evidence.reference_title: Imaging as an early biomarker to predict sensitivity to everolimus for progressive NF2-related vestibular schwannoma.
evidence.snippet: Pre-clinical studies highlighted the potential of mTORC1 inhibition in delaying schwannoma progression.
